coredata.dc:title: The fine-structure characteristics and isopropanol/water dehydration through pervaporation composite membranes improved with graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lecaros, Rumwald Leo G.
Chua, Krishellyn Y.
Tayo, Lemmuel L.
Hung, Wei-Song
Hu, Chien-Chieh
An, Quan-Fu
Tsai, Hui-An
Lee, Kueir-Rarn
Lai, Juin-Yih
coredata.dc:description: The facile synthesis and functionalization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown benefits in membrane separation processes since it could augment their performance. Here, the fine-structure characteristics and alcohol dehydration performance of a polymeric pervaporation composite membrane integrated with either nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) or oxygen-passivated graphene quantum dots (OGQDs) were evaluated. Calcium ion cross-linked sodium alginate (Alg) was used as the polymer matrix since the multiple hydroxyl and carboxyl groups could form hydrogen bonds with GQDs. The interfacial interaction between the two types of GQDs and Alg was evaluated through the pervaporation dehydration of isopropanol/water (i-PrOH/water) mixture and further analyzed using positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS). The PALS results revealed that different functionalized GQDs affect the fine-structure characteristics of Alg matrix and resulted to different alcohol dehydration performance. In dry state, both NGQD and OGQD integrated membranes have smaller free volume spaces than the pristine Alg membrane indicating improved interfacial interaction between the GQD and Alg. However, in wet state, larger free volume spaces appeared and it was shown that OGQD integrated membranes have the largest. These data explained the higher permeation flux and separation factor of Alg-OGQD membranes than Alg-NGQD and Alg. The total flux reached to 5580 g m−2h−1 with a water concentration in permeate of ~99.95% in dehydrating i-PrOH/water (70/30 wt%) at 70 °C for Alg-OGQD membrane with 100 ppm of OGQD (Alg-OGQD100). Furthermore, the membrane remained stable for 720 h of operation at 70 °C with a water concentration in permeate >99% showing potential for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Alcohol dehydration
Pervaporation
Free volume
Positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and membrane fabrication
Effect of feed temperature on the pervaporation separation performance
Effect of water concentration in feed on the pervaporation separation performance
Effect of GQD on the fine-structure of alginate membrane
Pervaporation performance of Alg-OGQD100 membrane in long-term operation and comparison with various membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis route of functionalized graphene quantum dots: Oxygen-passivated graphene quantum dots (OGQDs) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs).
Characterization of synthesized GQDs. (a) FTIR spectra; (b) XPS spectra; TEM image of (c) OGQD and (d) NGQD.
FESEM cross-sectional images of (a) Pristine Alg (b) Alg-NGQD30; (c) Alg-NGQD50; (d) Alg-NGQD100; (e) Alg-NGQD300; (f) Alg-NGQD500; and (g) Alg-OGQD100. Scale bar = 2.0 μm at ×20.0 k magnification.
Characterization of composite membranes: (a) FTIR and (b) XRD spectra.

Hydrophilicity of composite membranes: (a) Water contact angle and (b) degree of swelling of pristine Alg, Alg-NGQD and Alg-OGQD membranes. The swelling degree test was done by immersing the membranes in 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture for 24 h.

Pervaporation performance of alginate composite membranes with different NGQD loading amount: (a) Total flux and water concentration in permeate and (b) separation factor and pervaporation separation index (PSI). (Feed condition: 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture at 25 °C).

Comparison of NGQD and OGQD loaded membranes on the pervaporation separation: (a) Total flux and water concentration in permeate and (b) separation factor and pervaporation separation index (PSI). (Feed condition: 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture at 25 °C).

Effect of feed temperature on Alg-NGQD100 membrane: (a) Total flux and water concentration in permeate; (b) water and i-PrOH individual flux; (c) apparent activation energy of water and i-PrOH and (d) water and i-PrOH permeance and selectivity.

Effect of feed temperature on Alg-OGQD100 membrane: (a) Total flux and water concentration in permeate; (b) water and i-PrOH individual flux; (c) apparent activation energy of water and i-PrOH and (d) water and i-PrOH permeance and selectivity.

Effect of feed water concentration on the pervaporation performance at 70 °C of (a–) Alg-NGQD100 and (d–f) Alg-OGQD100 membranes: (a, d) Total flux and water concentration in permeate; (b, e) water and i-PrOH individual flux; and (c, f) water and i-PrOH individual permeance and selectivity.
Positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy results for the free volume radius and o-Ps lifetime distributions of both (a) dry and (b) wet state of Alg, Alg-NGQD100 and Alg-OGQD100 membranes.
Pervaporation performance of Alg-OGQD100 membrane for 720 h with 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture at 70 °C.
Free volume measurement of Alg, Alg-NGQD100 and Alg-OGQD100 membranes under dry state.
Free volume measurement of Alg, Alg-NGQD100 and Alg-OGQD100 membranes under wet state.
Comparison of pervaporation performance for i-PrOH/water mixtures with various membranes.

head.ce:title: The fine-structure characteristics and isopropanol/water dehydration through pervaporation composite membranes improved with graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Different functionalized graphene quantum dots resulted into different free-volume characteristics due to varying the formation of hydrogen bond with the polymer matrix.

The facile synthesis and functionalization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown benefits in membrane separation processes since it could augment their performance. Here, the fine-structure characteristics and alcohol dehydration performance of a polymeric pervaporation composite membrane integrated with either nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) or oxygen-passivated graphene quantum dots (OGQDs) were evaluated. Calcium ion cross-linked sodium alginate (Alg) was used as the polymer matrix since the multiple hydroxyl and carboxyl groups could form hydrogen bonds with GQDs. The interfacial interaction between the two types of GQDs and Alg was evaluated through the pervaporation dehydration of isopropanol/water (i-PrOH/water) mixture and further analyzed using positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS). The PALS results revealed that different functionalized GQDs affect the fine-structure characteristics of Alg matrix and resulted to different alcohol dehydration performance. In dry state, both NGQD and OGQD integrated membranes have smaller free volume spaces than the pristine Alg membrane indicating improved interfacial interaction between the GQD and Alg. However, in wet state, larger free volume spaces appeared and it was shown that OGQD integrated membranes have the largest. These data explained the higher permeation flux and separation factor of Alg-OGQD membranes than Alg-NGQD and Alg. The total flux reached to 5580 g m−2h−1 with a water concentration in permeate of ~99.95% in dehydrating i-PrOH/water (70/30 wt%) at 70 °C for Alg-OGQD membrane with 100 ppm of OGQD (Alg-OGQD100). Furthermore, the membrane remained stable for 720 h of operation at 70 °C with a water concentration in permeate >99% showing potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) has been widely used as an exceptional filler in preparing mixed matrix membranes. It has shown enhanced performance when incorporated in membranes for various separation processes like filtration or water treatment [1–3], gas separation [4–8] and pervaporation [7–9] due to its unique properties. Because of its 2-dimensional sheets, GO has an extended lateral dimension that tends to be distributed parallel on to the membrane surface. This structure will create a long tortuous path for the permeating molecules. The permeation rate would decrease but in return the selectivity towards the more polar molecules would enhance.

The mass transfer resistance for GO-incorporated or GO-based membranes has to be resolved in order to have better productivity. There are several methods to enhance the permeation with GO. This includes creating nano-sized pores or reducing the lateral size [10]. However, performing such additional process is tedious and cost-intensive especially for large-scale production of membranes. Membranologists are now searching for viable alternatives from nanoparticles that could offer ease in reproducibility and tunability. As such, an emerging nanofiller that resembles the structure and hydrophilicity of GO is the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [11]. It has been widely researched in biomedical [12–14], sensing [15–17] and optoelectronics [18–20] applications. GQDs have the unique sp2 carbon structure and hydrophilic functional groups of GO but confined in a nanoscale size ranging from ~2 to ~100 nm. It has been previously shown the enhancement of water selectivity of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) membrane when graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) were integrated during membrane preparation [21]. The permeation flux decreased, while the separation factor increased, at increasing GOQD loading. This proved that the mass transfer resistance was enhanced due to a more tortuous pathway but the membrane became more permselective towards water and rejects the transport of alcohol. This could be the effect of strong interfacial interaction between the PVA matrix and GOQD. In addition, the polarity of the filler tends to allow a more polar molecule to diffuse through the membrane. Wang et al. has also incorporated GOQDs in sodium alginate for the pervaporative dehydration of ethanol/water mixture but shows that due to shorter tortuous pathway the permeation flux increased [22]. In PVA system the only available intermolecular force present is H-bonding while in alginate both of H-bonding and electrostatic repulsion from the carboxyl groups. The electrostatic repulsion from the carboxyl groups present in alginate and GOQD may have caused the increase in free volume concentration leading to an increased permeation flux.

In order to explore the interaction between the GQD and polymer matrix, the GQDs were doped with nitrogen (NGQD) and then integrated into the alginate (Alg) matrix. The pervaporation performance of Alg-NGQD membranes by dehydrating isopropanol/water mixture is compared with membranes integrated with the oxygen-passivated GQD (OGQD). Since NGQDs will possess nitrogen functional groups it is hypothesized to have weaker H-bond but stronger electrostatic attraction with Alg than using OGQD due to the fact that hydroxyl groups form stronger H-bond. The fine-structure of pristine Alg, Alg-OGQD, and Alg-NGQD membranes is analyzed using positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) to get the free-volume characteristics information and is correlated to the pervaporation separation performance.

Calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets, and citric acid (CA) were purchased from SHOWA Chemical Industry Co., Ltd (Japan). The alginic acid (sodium salt) was purchased from Acros Organics (New Jersey, USA). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and ammonia water (28%) was obtained from Nihon Shiyaku Reagent (Japan). Methanol (HPLC grade, 99.9%) was purchased from Aencore Chemical Pty. Ltd. (Victoria, Australia). Isopropanol (99.9%) was obtained from Echo Chemical Co., Ltd. (Taiwan). The polyethersulfone (PES) support membranes were fabricated and provided by R&D Center for Membrane Technology, CYCU (Taiwan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and membrane fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs were synthesized using citric acid (CA) as the carbon source through moderate pyrolysis similar to the method of Dong et al. [11] with slight modifications. Briefly, 2 g of CA was weighed and placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 210 °C for 3 h followed by cooling it at room temperature naturally. The CA was liquefied signifying the formation of oxygen-passivated GQDs (OGQDs). The thick orange solution was neutralized to pH 7.0 by adding dropwise of 10 mg ml−1 NaOH solution under vigorous stirring and filling with deionized water until 100 mL. The aqueous dispersion was purified by dialysis using a CelluSep H1 dialysis tube with a MWCO of 2000 Da from Membrane Filtration Products Inc. (Seguin, Texas, USA) for 12 h. For the nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) [23], 0.3 mL of ammonia in water (28%) was added to CA prior to heating. The NGQDs were neutralized and dialyzed as the OGQD. The synthesized GQD was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator until use.

The pristine Alg membrane and with GQDs (NGQD or OGQD) were cast on the PES support membrane through solution casting method. The NGQDs at varying concentrations (30, 50, 100, 300, and 500 ppm) or OGQD (100 ppm) were dispersed in deionized water using an ultrasonicator. The alginate was added into the dispersed GQD suspension to make 1.5 wt% polymer solution at room temperature for 6 h under vigorous stirring. The undissolved substance was filtered then set aside to degas. The dope solution was cast on the PES support membrane with a 400 µm-gap casting knife. The membrane was dried at 60 °C for 2 h in an oven. The membranes were cooled at room temperature followed by immersing in 0.5 M CaCl2 bath to cross-link for 10 min. The cross-linked membranes were washed with methanol and deionized water for three times to remove excess unreacted CaCl2. After the cross-linking process, the membranes were vacuum-dried for 12 h at room temperature. The membranes were named as Alg-NGQDn or Alg-OGQDn, where “n” denotes the concentration of GQD in ppm. Cross-linked pristine Alg membranes were prepared for comparison. Homogeneous free-standing membranes were also prepared in glass plates with similar conditions described earlier for characterization.

The particles size of the synthesized GQDs were analyzed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai™ G2 F-20 S-TWIN). The chemical composition of GQDs was analyzed using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Thermo Scientific, K-Alpha) and the chemical functional group of the membranes and GQDs was acquired using a Fourier-transform infrared spectroscope (FTIR, Spectrum One, Perkin Elmer). X-ray diffraction (XRD, Model D8 Advance Eco, Bruker) at a scan range of 5° ≤ 2θ ≤ 30° was performed to characterize the amorphousness of the membrane. The cross-section morphology of the membranes was observed using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Model S-4800 SEM, Hitachi). While, the surface hydrophilicity of the membrane was analyzed by the measured water contact angle (WCA) using a contact angle goniometer (Model PV-DP, Face Kyowa Interface Science).

The swelling degree test was done by using the homogeneous dense free-standing membranes. The membranes were soaked in 70 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture for 24 h at 25 °C. The residual solvent on the membrane was immediately removed using delicate wipes. The swelling degree (SD, %) was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
SD =
W S
-
W D
W D
× 100 %
where WS and WD is the weight of the swollen and dried membranes, respectively.

The fine-structure and free volume information of the homogeneous dense free-standing pristine Alg, Alg-NGQD100 and Alg-OGQD100 membranes in dry and wet state was acquired through Positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS). The sample preparation and measurement were described in our previous study [24–28]. Briefly, the wet state membranes were pre-soaked in 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture. The free-volume radius (Rx ) is calculated using equation (2) correlating with the positron lifetime (τx ), where x denotes either 3 or 4:
(2)
τ x
=
1 2
1 -
R x
R x
+ Δ R
+
1
2 π
s i n
2 π
R x
R x
+ Δ R
- 1
where τx is in ns and Rx in Å, and ΔR is equal to 1.656 Å.

The R 3 and I 3 represents the radius and intensity of smaller free volume space (τ3) within the polymer chains. The R 4 and I 4 are the radius and intensity of the larger free volume space (τ4) brought by the interface between the polymer and filler as well as polymer chain aggregate.

The isopropanol/water mixtures were dehydrated through pervaporation (PV) as described previously [7,26,29,30]. Briefly, the membrane area of 5 cm2 was made in contact with the feed solution in a stainless steel membrane cell on a continuous flow set-up. The feed temperature was performed from 25 °C to 70 °C with a 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture. The feed concentration was adjusted to 10/90, 30/70, 50/50, 70/30, and 90/10 wt% i-PrOH/water mixtures at 70 °C.[31,32]

The permeate side was maintained under vacuum condition using a vacuum pump. The permeate was accumulated in a cold-trap with liquid nitrogen. The concentration of both feed and permeate was determined through gas chromatography equipped with TCD (GC, China Chromatography 8700 T). The total permeation flux (J, g m−2 h−1), pervaporation separation factor (αPV ), and pervaporation separation index (PSI) were calculated based on the following equations:
(3)
J =
W
A × t
(4)
α
PV
=
Y
Water
/
Y
i - P r O H
X
Water
/
X
i - P r O H
(5)
PSI = J × ( α - 1 )
where W (g) is the weight of the collected permeate at sampling time interval t (h). Y and X refers to the mass fraction of each component in permeate and feed, respectively.

The relationship of feed temperature and individual permeation flux (Ji , kg m−2 h−1) of each component i is determined through the Arrhenius equation given:
(6)
J i
= A
e
- E
p
/ R T
where A (kg m−2 h−1) is the pre-exponential factor; Ep (kJ mol−1) is the apparent activation energy of component i; R (kJ mol−1 K−1) is the ideal gas constant which is equivalent to 8.314 × 10−3 kJ mol−1 K−1 and T (K) is the operating feed temperature. The E p was obtained by plotting ln(Ji ) vs 1000/T.

Permeance (P/l, 1 GPU = 7.501 × 10−12 m3(STP) m−2 s−1 Pa−1) and selectivity (β) were calculated based on the following equations in order to normalize the effect of the driving force with the separation performance:
(7)
P / l
i
=
J i
p
i 0
-
p
il
=
J i
γ
i 0
x
i 0
p
i 0
sat
-
p
il
(8)
β =
P / l
Water
P / l
i - P r O H
where l (m) is the thickness of the membrane and Ji (g m−2 h−1) is the permeation flux of each component i (i-PrOH or water) and converted into volumes under standard temperature and pressure (STP). pi0 and pil (Pa) are the partial pressures of component i in the feed and permeate side but pil can be considered zero due to the high-vacuum conditions. xi0 is the mole fraction of component i in the feed solution, pi0 sat (Pa) is the saturated vapor pressure of pure component i, and γi0 is the activity coefficient of component i in the feed solution which is determined by Wilson equation using Aspen software (Aspen V9 Pro).

The synthesis of GQD from the partial pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) in the presence or absence of ammonia is shown in Fig. 1 . The oxygen-passivated GQDs (OGQDs) inherit the oxygen functional groups (e.g. carboxyl and hydroxyl) from citric acid. While the nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) possess additional nitrogen functional group (e.g. amine and graphitic-N) from a nitrogen source (i.e. ammonia).

The synthesized GQDs were characterized using FTIR, XPS and TEM as shown in Fig. 2 . The FTIR spectra (Fig. 2a) of both synthesized GQDs show the inherited oxygen-containing functional groups from citric acid such as CO and CO at 1385 cm−1 and 1558 cm−1, respectively. The broad OH stretch is also observed at ~3450 cm−1 and for the NGQDs with the overlapping NH stretch at the same region. The XPS spectra in Fig. 2b revealed that both synthesized GQDs are oxygen-rich. Though the nitrogen functional groups are not evident in FTIR spectrum, probably due to small content, the XPS spectrum for NGQDs shows the presence of nitrogen atoms which proves the successful doping. The hydroxyl, carboxyl, and nitrogen functional groups will form intermolecular forces (e.g. H-bonding and electrostatic attraction or repulsion) necessary to interact with sodium alginate matrix. The GQDs have a circular morphology and is a 0-dimensional particle while it has a thickness of ~1.2 nm as shown previously [21]. The TEM images in Fig. 2c-d shows the well-dispersed particles with a size ranging from 2 to 5 nm.

The cross-sectional morphology of the membranes is shown in Fig. 3 . The alginate was cast on the top surface of a PES supporting membrane. The pristine Alg composite membrane has a thickness of 635 ± 15 nm and integrating NGQD in increasing loading amount decreases the thickness to 495 ± 13 nm at 500 ppm. The thickness of the alginate layer with 100 ppm OGQD (Alg-OGQD100) was observed to be thinner than its NGQD counterpart. A comparable results was observed for sodium alginate [22] and PVA [21] membranes where the selective layer became thinner upon the addition of quantum dots. For similar reasons, the decrease in thickness may be due to the changes in the intermolecular attraction between the polymer matrix and added filler. No interfacial voids or defects could be observed due to the good compatibility between the filler and the polymer [33].

The effects of integrating GQDs on the chemical structure and amorphousness of the membrane were observed through FTIR and XRD spectra as shown in Fig. 4 . The OH stretching and the glycosidic bond (COC) of alginate can be observed at ~3400 cm−1 and 1096 cm−1, respectively. Another characteristic peak for alginate is observed at 1600 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 which is attributed to the carboxylate salt. The membranes integrated with 100 ppm of either NGQD or OGQD shows stronger CO stretching vibration (~1718 cm−1). The peak is higher for OGQD integrated membrane than NGQD indicating that OGQD possess more carbonyl or carboxyl groups and may affect the H-bonding with the alginate matrix. The diffraction peak of Alg can be observed at 2θ = 13°. The peak height declined after integrating GQDs which may indicate that more amorphous regions are created within the polymer matrix. The reason for the increased amorphousness is due to the hydrogen bond effect where the particles act as barriers for the alginate polymer chains to form H-bond to another alginate polymer chain. Thus, resulting to disturbances on the arrangement of alginate chains. These disturbances enlarge the interchain distance then creating more amorphous regions. The amorphous region is beneficial for the transport of permeating molecules.

The hydrophilicity of the membranes was observed through water contact angle (WCA) test as shown in Fig. 5 a. Alginate is hydrophilic in nature thus the decrease of WCA for membranes with alginate selective layer deposited onto the PES supporting membrane. The WCA of PES was at ~60° and declined to ~20° with Alg. The surface hydrophilicity was affected with increasing NGQD loading. The WCA slightly increased to ~25° which may indicate that reduction of hydrophilic groups exposed on surface. This may prevent excessive swelling of the membranes. Nevertheless, the WCA values still show that the Alg integrated with GQDs are hydrophilic enough to be water selective.

The pristine Alg membrane achieved the highest degree of swelling and it was significantly decreased after GQD integration (Fig. 5b). This further proves that interfacial interaction between the filler and polymer matrix exist resulting to restriction in membrane swelling.

The pervaporative dehydration of 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water at 25 °C was done in this study. The effects of NGQD loading amount is shown in Fig. 6 where the total flux was increased to 1822 g m−2h−1 and a water concentration in permeate of 99.70% for Alg-NGQD100 from the pristine Alg membranes with 1735 g m−2h−1 and 99.50%, respectively. The integration of NGQD may have provided interfacial spaces between the polymer chains and particles that allow faster diffusion of permeating molecules. The membranes are still water selective because both the alginate and NGQDs are hydrophilic. The total flux declined after 100 ppm of NGQD. The water concentration for Alg-NGQD300 was maintained at 99.64% while it decreased to 99.49% for Alg-NGQD500. Maintaining the water concentration in permeate and a decrease in total flux shows a more restricted path for water. A more restricted path may have also formed with larger interfacial voids in Alg-NGQD500 resulting to the decline of water concentration in permeate.

The separation factor and PSI value is presented in Fig. 6b. Both of the separation factor and PSI value increased from pristine Alg to Alg-NGQD100. Then, it decreased with increasing the loading amount to 300 and 500 ppm. The Alg-NGQD100 achieved the highest separation factor of 788 and PSI value of 1.43 × 106 g m−2 h−1 which is both almost twice as high as the pristine Alg membrane. Hence, the Alg-NGQD100 membrane was used to compare with Alg-OGQD100 membrane in separating i-PrOH/water mixtures.

The effect of the functional groups on the GQDs in the pervaporation separation performance of 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture at 25 °C is shown in Fig. 7 . The total flux for Alg-OGQD100 (1663 g m−2h−1) was lower than Alg-NGQD100 (1822 g m−2h−1) but the water concentration in permeate increased to ~99.9%. The significant change could be seen from the separation factor and PSI values. The pristine Alg membrane has the lowest separation factor and PSI value while a significant increase was observed for Alg-OGQD100 membranes. The Alg-OGQD100 membrane has a separation factor of 2331 and a PSI value of 3.87 × 106 g m−2 h−1 which is almost 3-folds increase compared with Alg-NGQD100 and almost 5-folds increase from the pristine Alg membranes. The results suggest that indeed the interfacial interaction between the polymer matrix and the added filler plays an important role in both permeation and permselectivity of the membrane. The weaker H-bonding and electrostatic attraction between NGQD and alginate led to an increase in permeation. While, the stronger H-bonding and electrostatic repulsion between OGQD and alginate resulted to a decline in permeation but the permselectivity significantly enhanced. From these observations it seems like the H-bonding has a greater effect over the electrostatic attraction. Contradicting with the XRD results, even though the Alg-OGQD100 membrane has created more amorphous regions the permeation flux decreases. The decline of permeation was brought about by a more restricted pathway due to stronger H-bonding. Possible changes in the free volume spaces occurred between the polymer chains and the GQDs.

Both sorption and diffusion contribute to the pervaporation separation process. The slight change in water contact angle indicated that the there is an insignificant change on the surface hydrophilicity of the membrane when integrated with GQDs. Therefore, the water sorption capability of pristine Alg and GQD integrated membranes is the same. Thus, resulting to increased flux and separation due to the possible formation of spaces that promotes water transport.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of feed temperature on the pervaporation separation performance
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of both types of GQDs was compared in changing the operating conditions of pervaporation separation. The effect of feed temperature on Alg-NGQD100 membrane is shown in Fig. 8 and for Alg-OGQD100 in Fig. 9 . The total flux for both types of membrane rapidly increased when the temperature was raised from 25 °C to 70 °C. The total flux increased ~2.8-folds for Alg-NGQD100 while ~3.4-folds for Alg-OGQD100. While the water concentration in permeate for Alg-NGQD100 slightly increased to ~99.9% and that the Alg-OGQD100 was able to maintain it above 99.9%.

The partial water and isopropanol fluxes are shown in Fig. 8b and 9b. The partial flux of water rapidly increased more than the i-PrOH. The reasons of this observation may come from [21]: (a) the thermal motion of the permeating molecules, thus promoting faster permeation; (b)the increase of partial pressure of each component in the feed side while the permeate side remained under vacuum condition, thus driving force is enhanced; and (c) increased polymer chain mobility [31,34], thus creating more free volume spaces. As observed, for both types of GQDs the i-PrOH flux was significantly lower compare to the water flux. This is the reason for the high water concentration in permeate.

The impact of temperature on the permeation flux can be quantitatively analyzed through the Arrhenius plot shown in Fig. 8c and 9c. In Alg-NGQD100, the apparent activation energy of water is 19.3 kJ/mol and 2.17 kJ/mol for i-PrOH. Similarly, in Alg-OGQD the apparent activation energy of water (22.7 kJ/mol) is higher than the i-PrOH (10.5 kJ/mol). The implication of the higher activation energy of water over i-PrOH is that the increase rate of water flux was higher than i-PrOH flux. The much higher water activation energy suggests that the water flux responds more with the increase of temperature resulting in the increase of separation. Fig. 8d and 9d shows the effect of feed temperature on permeance and selectivity with normalized driving force. The permeance of both permeating molecules decreases at high temperature due to the reduced solubility. Thus, the large enhancement in partial vapor pressure causes the increase of permeation flux. Comparing both types of GQDs, the Alg-OGQD100 membranes achieved a higher selectivity than Alg-NGQD100 due to a much lower i-PrOH permeance in the membranes integrated with OGQD. This is another indication of OGQD having stronger H-bond interaction with alginate. It is also noteworthy that the separation was maintained at high water concentration in permeate with increased flux at high temperature also showing the thermal stability of membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of water concentration in feed on the pervaporation separation performance
ce:section.ce:para: Changing the water concentration in feed was done at 70 °C because the membranes could maintain the water content in permeate at high concentration (~99.9%). Fig. 10 a-c shows the effect of water concentration in feed for Alg-NGQD100 membrane and Fig. 10d-f for Alg-OGQD100 membrane. The total flux for both types of membranes increased with increasing water content in feed. The individual fluxes of water and i-PrOH also increased. The plasticizing effect could be the reason due to the high hydrophilic character of the membrane. The membrane swells at high water content creating larger free volume spaces that will allow the transport of both permeating molecules through the membrane. It is also the reason for the decline of separation where the water concentration in permeate decreased. Normalizing the driving force, permeance and selectivity are also shown in Fig. 10c and f. The water permeance is much higher compared with i-PrOH permeance. The increased water permeance is because of the increased water partial pressure due to high concentration of water in feed. However, i-PrOH permeance also increases with water content in feed due to the plasticizing effect resulting to declined selectivity but the water concentration in permeate is still high enough at ~99.3% in feed concentration of 90/10 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture. Again, Alg-OGQD100 achieved the higher water selectivity due to lower i-PrOH flux.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GQD on the fine-structure of alginate membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in the fine-structure of the alginate matrix when GQDs are integrated under dry and wet conditions was analyzed through positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS). The membranes were immersed in the feed solution of 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water before testing for the wet condition. The free volume characteristics under dry condition are shown in Table 1 and wet condition in Table 2 while the free volume distribution is presented in Fig. 11 . The shorter o-Ps lifetime (τ3) was observed in both dry and wet states of membrane while a longer lifetime (τ4) was only found under wet condition. The R 3 and I 3 represents the radius and intensity of smaller free volume space (τ3) within the polymer chains. The R 4 and I 4 are the radius and intensity of the larger free volume space (τ4) brought by the interface between the polymer and filler as well as polymer chain aggregate. It was observed that both Alg-NGQD100 and Alg-OGQD100 membranes have smaller free volume radius but higher intensity than the pristine Alg membrane. The smaller free volume size when GQD was integrated shows a strong interaction with the polymer chain. An increased polymer chain interaction could be observed for Alg-OGQD100 which produced the smallest free volume size of 2.231 Å and highest concentration of 16.631%.

A more realistic correlation of the pervaporation separation performance with the free volume characteristic is under the wet condition since the membrane is in contact with the feed during the separation process. Interestingly, the small free volume spaces (τ3) from the dry state became even smaller under wet condition and larger free volume spaces appeared (τ4). The Alg-OGQD100 membrane has the largest free volume size of 5.423 Å followed by Alg-NGQD100 membrane (4.828 Å) then the pristine Alg membrane (4.101 Å). The smaller free volume spaces measured at τ3 in wet condition will coalesce and form a larger free volume that is measure at τ4. This happened due to the swelling of the membrane thus affecting the polymer chain packing. The OGQD integrated membranes have the largest free volume spaces at τ4 since most of the smaller free volume space may have coalesce in addition to the stronger H-bonding producing more interface between OGQD and alginate chains. This free volume information is directly associated with the trend of permeation flux at 70 °C (i.e. Alg-OGQD100 has the highest flux). On the other hand, the smaller free volume spaces (τ3) could be related to the selectivity of the membrane. Alg-OGQD100 has the smallest R 3 (1.860 Å) and hence has the highest water selectivity.

The results from PALS also confirmed that due to stronger H-bonding between OGQD and alginate it produced more interface between the polymer and filler as represented by τ4 but maintains the water permselectivity due to the suppression of polymer chain interaction which is represented by the information given by τ3. Indeed, the electrostatic attraction and weaker H-bonding between alginate and the nitrogen functional groups in NGQD produced smaller interfacial space between polymer and filler but larger free volume size in τ3 in which the water permselectivity of Alg-NGQD100 declined slightly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pervaporation performance of Alg-OGQD100 membrane in long-term operation and comparison with various membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The pervaporation test of Alg-OGQD100 membrane with 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture at 70 °C in long operating hours is presented in Fig. 12 to show the stability of the membrane. The total flux of the membrane was sustained at ~8000 g m−2h−1 after 72 h with a water concentration in permeate of >99% for 720 h at 70 °C. The prolonged contact of the membrane with the feed may have expanded the polymer chain distance resulting to the increased amorphousness and flux. However, it remained permselective towards water as the free volume spaces are small enough for the water to permeate. This shows that the alginate membranes integrated with OGQD is stable under long hours of operation at high temperature and feed water content.

The comparison of pervaporation performance is shown in Table 3 . The separation performance of the alginate membranes integrated with GQDs showed better separation at high water feed content of 30 wt% at 70 °C, showing good potential for alcohol dehydration through pervaporation.

The integration of different types of GQDs (OGQD and NGQD) based on its functional group had an effect on the properties and separating 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture through pervaporation. The OGQD was assumed to have more and stronger H-bonding with alginate than NGQD. The crystallinity of Alg-OGQD100 membrane was lower than Alg-NGQD100 showing more amorphous regions which is beneficial for transport. However, lower flux was achieved at 25 °C indicating a tortuous path may have formed but when the feed temperature was increased to 70 °C, the permeation flux of Alg-OGQD100 became higher than Alg-NGQD100. Another indication that interfacial interaction between polymer and filler had changed. The change is due to the effect of GQDs in the fine-structure of the membrane. The PALS results revealed the information for the free volume size and content in membranes. The Alg-OGQD100 membrane had created larger free volume size at longer o-Ps lifetime which is related to the polymer and filler interface. This is directly related to the increase in permeation flux when the feed temperature was raised. Lastly, the Alg-OGQD100 membrane showed good stability up to 720 h in separating 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixtures at 70 °C. The findings from this study provides information of the effect of interfacial interaction between polymer and filler on the pervaporation separation performance.

Rumwald Leo G. Lecaros: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Krishellyn Y. Chua: Methodology, Investigation. Lemmuel L. Tayo: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Wei-Song Hung: Supervision. Chien-Chieh Hu: Supervision. Quan-Fu An: Supervision. Hui-An Tsai: Supervision, Resources. Kueir-Rarn Lee: Supervision, Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Juin-Yih Lai: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to sincerely thank the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 106-2221-E-033-062-MY3) for financially supporting this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2020.116956.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
